nativeRange.habitat: Seasonal wetlands, marshes, and river margins in tropical East and Southeast Africa

decorPreferences: background placement with vertical room to grow
strong lighting to develop copper-bronze coloration
space stems apart so lower leaves receive adequate light

summary: A demanding red background stem plant from tropical East Africa with striking copper-bronze to reddish-brown narrow leaves; develops its showcase coloration only in high-tech planted tanks with strong light, CO2, and iron-rich fertilization.
careNotes: Nesaea crassicaulis is a stem plant native to tropical East and Southeast Africa, sold in the hobby for its bold reddish-brown to copper-bronze narrow leaves on thick reddish stems — a less common sight in the hobby than its sister Golden Nesea (Nesaea pedicellata, fw-plant-133) or the Ammannia gracilis / senegalensis trio. Modern taxonomy is in flux: the Graham & Gandhi (2013) revision folds the entire genus Nesaea into Ammannia, so this plant is increasingly listed as Ammannia crassicaulis (Guill. & Perr.) Koehne; both names appear in current retail and care guides and the file records the hobby-current Nesaea crassicaulis as primary with the Ammannia synonym in alsoKnownAs. Sources broadly agree on a temperature range of 22-28 °C and tolerance of soft-to-moderately-hard water (gH ~2-12 dGH). pH bounds disagree slightly: Buce Plant cites a tighter pH 6.0-7.0 and 'soft water preferred', while Aqua-Fish.net allows pH 6.0-8.0 with 0-12 dGH; the file uses a conservative pH 6.0-7.5 spanning both. Adult height is the other point of cross-source disagreement: Buce Plant lists 6-12 in (~15-30 cm), likely shipped size, while Aqua-Fish.net describes 30-50 cm at full submerged height; the file uses a 15-50 cm range to span both shipped and mature in-tank dimensions. To develop the characteristic copper-bronze to reddish-brown coloration, the plant generally needs high light (Aqua-Fish.net cites 0.5-1 W/L; equivalent to ~30-50 PAR), pressurized CO2 (recommended though not strictly mandatory), and consistent iron + micronutrient dosing alongside macros. Under low light it survives but stays dull green and tends to drop lower leaves. The plant prefers a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil or root tabs) and is sensitive to abrupt parameter swings — sudden changes can trigger melt, especially on freshly planted tissue-culture stems transitioning from emersed to submersed growth.
breedingNotes: Propagated by stem cuttings: cut healthy side shoots or stem tops (about 5-10 cm with several nodes) from a mature stem, strip leaves from the lower nodes, and replant in nutrient-rich substrate. Trimming the top of an established stem encourages bushier lateral side-shoot growth on the mother plant. Spacing stems apart at planting prevents shading of lower leaves and helps maintain compact, fully-clothed stems. Seed propagation is not used in the hobby.

primary.name: Buce Plant
primary.notes: Used for family Lythraceae, scientific name Nesaea crassicaulis, adult height 6-12 in (15-30 cm shipped/young), pH 6.0-7.0, soft water preferred, moderate-to-high lighting, CO2 recommended, fast growth, background placement, medium care difficulty, stem-cutting propagation, nutrient-rich substrate, sensitivity to parameter swings, reddish-brown narrow-leaf coloration, and 'rarer sight in the hobby' note.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net
additional.notes: Cross-checked native range (eastern and southeastern Africa), adult size 30-50 cm at full submerged height, temperature 22-28 C, pH 6.0-8.0 (wider than Buce Plant), hardness 0-12 dGH, strong lighting requirement (0.5-1 W/L), CO2 highly recommended, fine gravel substrate, nutrient-rich water and root + liquid fertilization, stem-cutting propagation from side shoots and stem tops, medium-to-high difficulty.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database
additional.notes: Used to confirm taxonomy: family Lythraceae, order Myrtales, full scientific authority Nesaea crassicaulis (Guill. & Perr.) Koehne, and synonym Ammannia crassicaulis — supporting the Graham & Gandhi (2013) Nesaea-into-Ammannia revision noted across the genus.
